method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a mostly submarine volcano on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km above the seafloor and extends about 20 km across, with only small portions historically emergent above sea level.[4] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, whose emergent northern and western rim segments formed the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4]

Early geological work described Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai as elongated islands lying tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga consisted of alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping gently away from the inferred centre, supporting the interpretation that the islands were remnants of a submarine caldera rim.[2] Later high-resolution multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the anticipated caldera site, consistent with explosive collapse during an earlier eruption.[2] The volcano’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4]

The 2014–2015 eruption built a tephra cone that joined Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] The new island was small, uninhabited and short-lived, covering just over 1 km² before the 2022 eruption.[5] The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island, leaving only small remnants of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean surface.[4][5] The 2022 event has been described as a once-in-1,000-years eruption for the Hunga caldera and could rank as high as VEI 5, although further analysis was required to confirm the erupted volume.[5]

==Geography==
The above-water island was uninhabited and lay in Tonga’s Haʻapai island group, about 65 km north of Tongatapu and about 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou.[4] Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m above sea level.[4] Its short-lived land surface was closely monitored by satellite because rapid erosion of fresh volcanic deposits provided a useful natural analogue for interpreting similar landforms on Mars.[5] Following the 2022 eruption, the former island was reduced to very small subaerial fragments at its western and eastern edges.[4] Ash from the eruption was dispersed across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua island groups, with reported deposits of about 20–30 mm on Tongatapu and thicker accumulations elsewhere.[4]

==Footnotes==
The 2022 event has been described in scientific literature as one of the largest volcanic eruptions observed with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated volcanic explosivity index of about 6.[1] Reported estimates include an eruption column reaching up to 57 km, tephra dispersed across an area at least 600 km in diameter, and about 2.9 × 10^15 g of basaltic-andesitic material released into the surrounding ocean region.[1] Contemporary scientific commentary identified a possible submarine landslide as a trigger for the eruption’s extreme explosivity, by removing overlying material and allowing rapid decompression.[2] The eruption generated tsunami waves recorded across the Pacific, and later studies emphasized its importance for understanding both volcanic tsunamis and atmosphere-driven meteotsunamis.[2][3] Subsequent atmospheric studies found that the eruption injected exceptional amounts of water vapour into the stratosphere, producing cooling in the mid-stratosphere and warming in the lower stratosphere for more than a year.[4] Other research concluded that the eruption caused a small net cooling in the Southern Hemisphere, partly because sulfate aerosols and ozone changes offset the expected warming influence of the added stratospheric water vapour.[5]

==Eruptions==
The climactic phase of the 2022 eruption began at about 5 p.m. local time on 15 January and produced violent explosions that sent gas and ash high into the atmosphere.[4] The eruption occurred from a shallow submarine vent, about 200 m below the ocean surface, and injected large quantities of water vapour along with a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere.[1] Satellite-era analyses described the plume as rising more than 30 km, with overshooting tops above 55 km, and estimated the initial explosive energy release at 10–28 exajoules.[5]

Researchers attributed the eruption’s exceptional explosivity to the interaction of very hot magma, typically exceeding 1,000 °C, with seawater of about 20 °C in a confined volcanic setting.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin described the event as unlike a standard Surtseyan eruption, because the volume of water involved was unusually large, and some volcanologists informally referred to it as an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption.[3] One proposed explanation was that weakening or partial collapse of the northern caldera rim allowed large volumes of seawater to enter an underground magma chamber, rapidly generating steam and fragmentation.[3] The absence of seismometers on the volcano limited direct reconstruction of the precursory activity, and the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles away at the time of the eruption.[4] In response, the United States Geological Survey’s National Earthquake Information Center and Volcano Disaster Assistance Program developed methods to identify small earthquakes near the volcano and assemble a near-real-time post-eruption earthquake catalogue.[4]

The eruption generated destructive tsunamis in Tonga, where run-up reached as much as 65 ft above sea level, hundreds of structures were destroyed, more than 1,500 people were displaced, and four deaths were reported.[4] Tsunami waves also affected other Pacific coasts, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia and New Zealand.[4] Marine surveys after the eruption found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples up to 50 km from the volcano, as well as gouged valleys and large sediment accumulations on the seafloor.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable, which was severed during the eruption, was found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4] Scientists estimated that up to one and two-thirds cubic miles of material had been displaced, with additional deposits likely remaining unmapped.[4]

The eruption had unusual atmospheric effects because it injected both sunlight-reflecting sulfate aerosols and climate-warming water vapour into the stratosphere.[1][2] The sulfur dioxide was rapidly converted into sulfate aerosols, which tend to cool the atmosphere by reflecting incoming solar radiation.[1] At the same time, the submarine eruption increased total stratospheric water content by about 10%, an unprecedented perturbation in the modern observational record.[2] Although early concern focused on possible warming from the added water vapour, a UCLA-led study found that the eruption instead reduced Southern Hemisphere temperatures by about 0.1 °C and produced a slight net global cooling from 2022 onward.[1] The same study noted indications of a very small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere, where stratospheric water vapour can persist for years.[1] Researchers emphasized that the event demonstrated how shallow submarine eruptions can produce complex atmospheric responses involving both aerosols and water vapour.[1]

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a mostly submarine volcano on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km above the seafloor and extends about 20 km across, with only small portions historically emergent above sea level.[4] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, whose emergent northern and western rim segments formed the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4]

Early geological work described Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai as elongated islands lying tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Samples from Hunga Tonga consisted of alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping gently away from the inferred centre, supporting the interpretation that the islands were remnants of a submarine caldera rim.[2] Later high-resolution multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the anticipated caldera site, consistent with explosive collapse during an earlier eruption.[2] The volcano’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4]

The above-water island was uninhabited and lay in Tonga’s Haʻapai island group, about 65 km north of Tongatapu and about 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou.[4] Before the January 2022 eruption, the joined island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m above sea level.[4] Its short-lived land surface was closely monitored by satellite because rapid erosion of fresh volcanic deposits provided a useful natural analogue for interpreting similar landforms on Mars.[5] Following the 2022 eruption, the former island was reduced to very small subaerial fragments at its western and eastern edges.[4] Ash from the eruption was dispersed across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua island groups, with reported deposits of about 20–30 mm on Tongatapu and thicker accumulations elsewhere.[4]

The climactic phase of the 2022 eruption began at about 5 p.m. local time on 15 January and produced violent explosions that sent gas and ash high into the atmosphere.[4] The eruption occurred from a shallow submarine vent, about 200 m below the ocean surface, and injected large quantities of water vapour along with a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere.[1] Satellite-era analyses described the plume as rising more than 30 km, with overshooting tops above 55 km, and estimated the initial explosive energy release at 10–28 exajoules.[5]
